section: Biological and Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems
provenance: Original content. Concepts calibrated against MCAT QBook Ch1 (Biology) + Ch2 (Biochemistry) used privately as ground truth; no source text reproduced.
trademark_notice: MCAT is a registered trademark of the AAMC. This material is independently authored and not endorsed by the AAMC.

passages.title: Fidelity of eukaryotic DNA replication
passages.passage_text: A cell biologist studies how a cultured human cell line copies its genome before division. At each replication origin, the double helix is opened and the two parental strands each serve as a template. One new strand is built continuously toward the moving fork, while the other is assembled in short pieces that are later joined. The researcher also examines how the cell corrects rare base-pairing errors that escape the polymerase during synthesis.

questions.stem: The observation that each daughter duplex contains one parental strand and one newly synthesized strand indicates that replication is:

options.A: Conservative

questions.explanation: Semiconservative replication produces two duplexes that each retain one intact parental strand paired with a new strand, which is exactly what the described outcome shows.

distractor_notes.A: Conservative replication would leave the original duplex fully intact and produce a wholly new duplex, contradicting the mixed strands observed.
distractor_notes.B: Dispersive replication would scatter old and new segments within a single strand, not keep one parental strand whole.
distractor_notes.D: Bidirectional replication describes the direction of fork movement, not the strand composition of daughter duplexes.

questions.ground_truth_ref: semiconservative replication (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: The strand assembled in short pieces that are later joined is the lagging strand. The enzyme that seals the remaining nicks between adjacent fragments is:

options.A: Helicase

questions.explanation: After the RNA primers are replaced with DNA, DNA ligase catalyzes formation of the phosphodiester bond that joins adjacent Okazaki fragments, sealing the nick in the lagging strand.

distractor_notes.A: Helicase unwinds the parental duplex ahead of the fork; it does not join fragments.
distractor_notes.B: Primase lays down the short RNA primers that start each fragment; it does not seal them together.
distractor_notes.C: Topoisomerase relieves supercoiling ahead of the fork; it does not ligate fragments.

questions.ground_truth_ref: DNA ligase and Okazaki fragments (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: The lagging strand is synthesized discontinuously rather than continuously because DNA polymerase:

options.A: Reads the template in the 5' to 3' direction
options.C: Can add nucleotides only to the 3' end of a growing strand
options.D: Cannot use a primer on the lagging strand

questions.explanation: DNA polymerase can only extend a strand in the 5' to 3' direction, adding nucleotides to a free 3'-hydroxyl. Because the two template strands are antiparallel, the strand running the wrong way relative to fork movement must be copied in short 5'-to-3' pieces (Okazaki fragments).

distractor_notes.A: Polymerase reads the template 3' to 5'; describing it as reading 5' to 3' misstates the mechanism and does not explain discontinuity.
distractor_notes.B: DNA synthesis does not require molecular oxygen.
distractor_notes.D: Each Okazaki fragment does begin with a primer; the constraint is the polymerase's fixed 5'-to-3' direction, not an inability to use primers.

questions.ground_truth_ref: 5'-to-3' synthesis and lagging strand (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: A base that is misincorporated and escapes proofreading during synthesis is later recognized and removed by mismatch repair. If this repair system is defective, the most likely consequence is:

options.A: Complete failure to initiate replication
options.B: Loss of the ability to unwind the helix
options.C: Immediate arrest of the cell in G0 with no further effects
options.D: An increased spontaneous mutation rate in the genome

questions.explanation: Mismatch repair corrects base-pairing errors that the polymerase's proofreading missed. Losing this system leaves those errors uncorrected, so they become fixed mutations after the next round of replication, raising the mutation rate.

distractor_notes.A: Replication still initiates without mismatch repair; only the correction of errors is lost.
distractor_notes.B: Helix unwinding is performed by helicase and is independent of mismatch repair.
distractor_notes.C: Loss of mismatch repair does not force cells into permanent arrest; it typically allows continued division with accumulating mutations.

questions.ground_truth_ref: mismatch repair and mutation rate (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: In the absence of telomerase, the ends of linear chromosomes in these somatic cells would be expected to:

options.A: Shorten progressively with each round of replication
options.C: Remain exactly the same length indefinitely
options.D: Circularize to avoid any end problem

questions.explanation: Because the lagging strand cannot be completed all the way to the 5' end after the terminal primer is removed, linear chromosomes lose a small amount of end sequence each division. Without telomerase to extend the telomeres, they shorten over successive rounds.

distractor_notes.B: Lengthening requires telomerase activity, which is absent here.
distractor_notes.C: The end-replication problem guarantees gradual loss, so the length does not stay constant without telomerase.
distractor_notes.D: Human somatic chromosomes remain linear; they do not circularize to solve the end problem.

questions.ground_truth_ref: end-replication problem / telomeres (QBook Ch1) - original framing

passages.title: Cell-cycle checkpoints and tumor suppression
passages.passage_text: A dividing cell progresses through G1, S, G2, and M phases under the control of regulatory checkpoints. At the boundary between G1 and S, the cell verifies that its DNA is undamaged before committing to replication. A laboratory studies a tumor cell line in which a key checkpoint protein that normally halts the cycle in response to DNA damage is nonfunctional.

questions.stem: In which phase of the cell cycle is the nuclear DNA content of the cell replicated?

options.A: G1 phase

questions.explanation: DNA synthesis (replication) occurs during S phase, doubling the cell's DNA content in preparation for division.

distractor_notes.A: G1 is a growth/gap phase before DNA synthesis begins.
distractor_notes.B: G2 is a growth/gap phase after replication, during which the cell prepares for mitosis.
distractor_notes.D: M phase is when the duplicated chromosomes are segregated and the cell divides, not when DNA is copied.

questions.ground_truth_ref: S phase DNA synthesis (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: The G1/S checkpoint primarily ensures that:

options.A: Sister chromatids have properly attached to the spindle
options.C: The nuclear envelope has reformed after mitosis
options.D: The cell is large enough and its DNA is intact before replication begins

questions.explanation: The G1/S checkpoint verifies that conditions are favorable, the cell has grown sufficiently, and the DNA is undamaged before the cell commits to copying its genome in S phase.

distractor_notes.A: Spindle attachment of sister chromatids is monitored at the spindle-assembly (M) checkpoint, not G1/S.
distractor_notes.B: Completion of cytokinesis is an M-phase event that follows the checkpoint in question.
distractor_notes.C: Reformation of the nuclear envelope occurs during telophase of mitosis, not at G1/S.

questions.ground_truth_ref: G1/S checkpoint function (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: The nonfunctional checkpoint protein in this tumor line normally arrests the cycle when DNA is damaged. Its loss most directly promotes cancer because damaged cells:

options.A: Proceed through the cycle without repairing damage, accumulating mutations
options.B: Are prevented from ever entering S phase
options.C: Increase their rate of apoptosis and die off

questions.explanation: A checkpoint protein that halts the cycle in response to damage acts as a tumor suppressor. Without it, cells with DNA damage continue dividing, so mutations accumulate and can drive unregulated proliferation.

distractor_notes.B: Losing the arrest signal allows entry into S phase; it does not block it.
distractor_notes.C: The lost function normally can trigger arrest or apoptosis; its absence reduces, not increases, protective cell death.
distractor_notes.D: Loss of a suppressor promotes continued division rather than permanent arrest.

questions.ground_truth_ref: tumor suppressor checkpoint loss (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: A gene whose normal product restrains cell division, and whose loss-of-function promotes cancer, is best classified as a:

options.A: Proto-oncogene

questions.explanation: Tumor suppressor genes normally restrain proliferation; cancer arises when their function is lost (typically requiring inactivation of both alleles). The described checkpoint gene fits this definition.

distractor_notes.A: A proto-oncogene normally promotes division and becomes harmful when hyperactivated, the opposite regulatory role.
distractor_notes.C: An oncogene drives cancer through a gain of function, whereas the described gene contributes to cancer when its function is lost.
distractor_notes.D: Housekeeping genes maintain basal cellular functions and are not defined by restraining division.

questions.ground_truth_ref: tumor suppressor vs oncogene (QBook Ch1) - original framing

passages.title: Reciprocal control of glycogen metabolism and gluconeogenesis
passages.passage_text: In the liver, glucose can be stored as glycogen after a meal and released into the blood during fasting. Glycogen synthesis and glycogen breakdown are catalyzed by different enzymes that are reciprocally regulated so that they are not maximally active at the same time. During prolonged fasting, hepatocytes also synthesize new glucose from noncarbohydrate precursors. A biochemistry group tracks how covalent modification and hormone signaling switch these pathways on and off.

questions.stem: The rate-limiting enzyme that releases glucose units from stored glycogen is:

options.A: Glycogen synthase

questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase catalyzes the phosphorolytic cleavage of alpha-1,4 bonds, releasing glucose-1-phosphate from glycogen; it is the key regulated enzyme of glycogen breakdown.

distractor_notes.A: Glycogen synthase builds glycogen; it is the enzyme of synthesis, not breakdown.
distractor_notes.B: Hexokinase phosphorylates free glucose entering glycolysis, not glycogen breakdown.
distractor_notes.D: Pyruvate kinase acts at the end of glycolysis and is unrelated to glycogen mobilization.

questions.ground_truth_ref: glycogen phosphorylase in glycogenolysis (QBook Ch2) - original framing

questions.stem: Glucagon signaling raises cAMP and activates protein kinase A, which phosphorylates both key glycogen enzymes. The net effect of this phosphorylation is to:

options.A: Activate glycogen synthase and inhibit glycogen phosphorylase
options.B: Activate glycogen phosphorylase and inhibit glycogen synthase

questions.explanation: Phosphorylation activates glycogen phosphorylase and inactivates glycogen synthase, so during fasting the liver breaks down glycogen and releases glucose while shutting off storage. This reciprocal control prevents a futile cycle.

distractor_notes.A: This reverses the actual pattern; phosphorylation turns storage off and breakdown on.
distractor_notes.C: Activating both at once would create a futile cycle, which reciprocal regulation is designed to avoid.
distractor_notes.D: Inhibiting both would prevent glucose release when it is needed during fasting.

questions.ground_truth_ref: reciprocal phosphorylation of glycogen enzymes (QBook Ch2) - original framing

questions.stem: Gluconeogenesis is not simply glycolysis run in reverse because three glycolytic steps are essentially irreversible. To bypass them, gluconeogenesis uses distinct enzymes. Which enzyme is unique to gluconeogenesis?

options.A: Phosphofructokinase-1

questions.explanation: Glucose-6-phosphatase hydrolyzes glucose-6-phosphate to free glucose, bypassing the hexokinase step. It is a gluconeogenic enzyme (found mainly in liver) with no role in glycolysis.

distractor_notes.A: Phosphofructokinase-1 is a committed glycolytic enzyme; gluconeogenesis instead uses fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase.
distractor_notes.B: Hexokinase is a glycolytic enzyme that traps glucose; it is not unique to gluconeogenesis.
distractor_notes.C: Pyruvate kinase is a glycolytic enzyme; the gluconeogenic bypass uses pyruvate carboxylase and PEP carboxykinase.

questions.ground_truth_ref: gluconeogenesis bypass enzymes (QBook Ch2) - original framing

questions.stem: Running glycolysis and gluconeogenesis at full speed at the same time would accomplish nothing except net hydrolysis of ATP. Preventing this outcome is the purpose of:

options.A: Substrate-level phosphorylation
options.D: Feedback activation of both pathways by ATP

questions.explanation: Reciprocal regulation ensures that when one pathway is active the opposing pathway is suppressed, avoiding a futile cycle that would waste ATP without net product.

distractor_notes.A: Substrate-level phosphorylation is a mechanism of ATP formation, not a way to prevent opposing pathways from running together.
distractor_notes.C: Codon degeneracy concerns the genetic code and is unrelated to metabolic futile cycles.
distractor_notes.D: ATP does not activate both pathways; it signals energy abundance and favors storage while suppressing breakdown.

questions.ground_truth_ref: futile cycle avoidance / reciprocal regulation (QBook Ch2) - original framing

questions.stem: The liver, but not skeletal muscle, can release free glucose into the bloodstream from its glycogen stores. This difference exists because skeletal muscle lacks:

options.A: Glucose-6-phosphatase

questions.explanation: Muscle can break glycogen down to glucose-6-phosphate but lacks glucose-6-phosphatase, so it cannot dephosphorylate glucose for export. It therefore uses the glucose internally rather than releasing it to the blood, a role reserved for the liver.

distractor_notes.B: Muscle does have glycogen phosphorylase and readily breaks down its own glycogen.
distractor_notes.C: Muscle has glycogen synthase and stores glycogen; that is not the limiting difference.
distractor_notes.D: Muscle has glucose transporters (e.g., GLUT4); the barrier to export is the missing phosphatase, not transport.

questions.ground_truth_ref: liver vs muscle glucose-6-phosphatase (QBook Ch2) - original framing

passages.title: Acid-base behavior of an amino acid
passages.passage_text: A student titrates a solution of the neutral amino acid alanine, which has an alpha-carboxyl group with a pKa near 2.3 and an alpha-amino group with a pKa near 9.7. Starting from a strongly acidic solution, base is added slowly while the pH is monitored. The student wants to relate the observed ionization states to the net charge on the molecule and to identify the pH at which alanine carries no net charge.

questions.stem: In a strongly acidic solution (pH well below 2), alanine exists predominantly with:

options.A: A protonated amino group (positive) and a protonated carboxyl group (neutral), giving a net charge of +1
options.B: A deprotonated amino group and a deprotonated carboxyl group, giving a net charge of -1
options.C: A neutral amino group and a deprotonated carboxyl group, giving a net charge of 0
options.D: Both groups deprotonated, giving a net charge of -2

questions.explanation: At a pH far below both pKa values, both ionizable groups are protonated: the amino group is -NH3+ (positive) and the carboxyl is -COOH (neutral). The net charge is therefore +1.

distractor_notes.B: Full deprotonation of both groups occurs at high pH, not in strongly acidic solution.
distractor_notes.C: A net-zero species requires the carboxyl to be deprotonated, which happens only above its pKa.
distractor_notes.D: A -2 species is impossible for a simple amino acid with only these two ionizable groups.

questions.ground_truth_ref: amino acid protonation states (QBook Ch2) - original values

questions.stem: For this amino acid, whose only ionizable groups are the alpha-carboxyl and alpha-amino groups, the isoelectric point (pI) is approximately:
questions.explanation: For an amino acid with no ionizable side chain, the pI is the average of the two relevant pKa values: (2.3 + 9.7)/2 = 6.0. At this pH the molecule exists mainly as the zwitterion with no net charge.

distractor_notes.A: 2.3 is the carboxyl pKa, the pH of half-dissociation of that group, not the pI.
distractor_notes.C: 9.7 is the amino-group pKa, not the isoelectric point.
distractor_notes.D: 12.0 lies above both pKa values, where the molecule carries a net negative charge.

questions.ground_truth_ref: pI as average of flanking pKa (QBook Ch2) - original values

questions.stem: At the pH equal to the carboxyl pKa (about 2.3), the carboxyl group is:

options.A: Fully protonated

questions.explanation: By definition, when the pH equals the pKa of a group, the protonated and deprotonated forms are present in equal amounts (a 1:1 ratio by the Henderson-Hasselbalch relationship).

distractor_notes.A: Full protonation occurs well below the pKa, not exactly at it.
distractor_notes.B: Full deprotonation occurs well above the pKa.
distractor_notes.D: Titration changes the protonation state; it does not convert a carboxyl group into an amino group.

questions.ground_truth_ref: pH = pKa half-dissociation (QBook Ch2) - original framing

questions.stem: If alanine's side chain (a methyl group) were replaced by a carboxyl-containing side chain (as in aspartate), the pI of the resulting amino acid would be expected to:

options.A: Rise well above 6, becoming basic
options.B: Stay exactly 6.0, since side chains do not affect pI
options.C: Become undefined because the molecule can no longer be zwitterionic

questions.explanation: An acidic side chain adds a low-pKa ionizable group, so the net-neutral point is reached at a lower pH. The pI of an acidic amino acid is the average of its two lowest (acidic) pKa values and lies well below 6.

distractor_notes.A: Adding an acidic group lowers, not raises, the pI; basic amino acids (with an extra amino group) have high pI.
distractor_notes.B: Ionizable side chains do change the pI; only side chains without ionizable groups leave it as the average of the two backbone pKa values.
distractor_notes.C: The molecule still has a net-neutral pH; adding an acidic group shifts the pI rather than eliminating it.

questions.ground_truth_ref: effect of acidic side chain on pI (QBook Ch2) - original framing

passages.title: Gas exchange and the control of breathing
passages.passage_text: In the lungs, air reaches millions of thin-walled alveoli surrounded by pulmonary capillaries. Oxygen and carbon dioxide cross the alveolar-capillary membrane by diffusion. A physiology class examines how gases move between alveolar air and blood, how carbon dioxide is carried in the blood, and how the brainstem adjusts ventilation in response to blood chemistry.

questions.stem: Oxygen moves from alveolar air into pulmonary capillary blood because:

options.A: The partial pressure of oxygen is higher in the alveoli than in the incoming capillary blood
options.B: Oxygen is actively pumped across the alveolar membrane using ATP
options.C: Blood in the pulmonary capillaries is already fully oxygenated
options.D: Carbon dioxide pushes oxygen across the membrane

questions.explanation: Gas exchange is passive diffusion down partial-pressure gradients. Alveolar oxygen partial pressure exceeds that of the deoxygenated blood arriving in the pulmonary capillaries, so oxygen diffuses into the blood.

distractor_notes.B: Alveolar gas exchange is diffusion down a gradient, not ATP-driven active transport.
distractor_notes.C: Blood arriving at the lungs is deoxygenated; if it were already saturated, no net uptake would occur.
distractor_notes.D: Carbon dioxide diffuses along its own gradient and does not push oxygen across the membrane.

questions.ground_truth_ref: alveolar diffusion gradients (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: Most carbon dioxide is transported in the blood in the form of:

options.A: Dissolved CO2 gas in the plasma
options.B: Bicarbonate ion formed after CO2 combines with water

questions.explanation: The majority of CO2 is carried as bicarbonate. In red blood cells, carbonic anhydrase catalyzes CO2 + H2O to carbonic acid, which dissociates into bicarbonate and a proton; the bicarbonate is exported to the plasma.

distractor_notes.A: Only a small fraction of CO2 travels as dissolved gas in plasma.
distractor_notes.C: Carbaminohemoglobin carries some CO2 but is a minority pathway compared with bicarbonate.
distractor_notes.D: Carbon monoxide is a toxic gas, not the physiological carrier of CO2.

questions.ground_truth_ref: CO2 transport as bicarbonate (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: The single most important chemical stimulus that increases the rate and depth of breathing in a healthy person is a rise in arterial:

options.A: Oxygen partial pressure
options.C: Carbon dioxide partial pressure (and the associated fall in pH)

questions.explanation: Central and peripheral chemoreceptors are most sensitive to rising CO2 and the resulting drop in blood/cerebrospinal-fluid pH. This is the primary drive that stimulates increased ventilation to blow off CO2.

distractor_notes.A: A rise in oxygen would reduce, not increase, respiratory drive; low oxygen is only a secondary stimulus.
distractor_notes.B: Blood glucose is not the primary chemical regulator of ventilation.
distractor_notes.D: Plasma sodium does not directly drive the respiratory rate.

questions.ground_truth_ref: CO2/pH chemoreceptor drive (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: A person voluntarily hyperventilates at rest, blowing off large amounts of CO2. The most likely immediate effect on arterial pH is that it will:

options.A: Fall, producing a respiratory acidosis
options.C: Fall because bicarbonate is added to the blood

questions.explanation: Removing CO2 shifts the CO2 + H2O to bicarbonate + H+ equilibrium to the left, lowering H+ concentration. Fewer protons means a higher pH, i.e., respiratory alkalosis.

distractor_notes.A: Acidosis (falling pH) results from CO2 retention, the opposite of hyperventilation.
distractor_notes.B: Removing CO2 does change the equilibrium and therefore the pH.
distractor_notes.C: Blowing off CO2 consumes carbonic acid/bicarbonate rather than adding bicarbonate, and it raises pH.

questions.ground_truth_ref: hyperventilation and respiratory alkalosis (QBook Ch1) - original framing

passages.title: Filtration and reabsorption in the nephron
passages.passage_text: The functional unit of the kidney is the nephron. Blood is filtered at the glomerulus into Bowman's capsule, and the filtrate then flows through the proximal tubule, the loop of Henle, the distal tubule, and the collecting duct. Along the way, the composition of the filtrate is adjusted by selective reabsorption and secretion, and its final concentration is regulated by hormones. A renal physiology group traces the handling of water and solutes along this path.

questions.stem: At the glomerulus, the driving force that pushes fluid and small solutes out of the blood and into Bowman's capsule is primarily:

options.A: Hydrostatic (blood) pressure in the glomerular capillaries
options.B: Active transport of plasma across the capillary wall
options.C: The osmotic pull of filtrate already in the tubule
options.D: Diffusion of large plasma proteins into the capsule

questions.explanation: Glomerular filtration is driven by the high hydrostatic pressure of blood in the glomerular capillaries, which forces water and small solutes through the filtration barrier into Bowman's capsule.

distractor_notes.B: Filtration at the glomerulus is a pressure-driven bulk process, not active transport of plasma.
distractor_notes.C: Filtrate downstream does not pull fluid out of the glomerulus; the net force is the capillary hydrostatic pressure.
distractor_notes.D: Large plasma proteins are normally retained in the blood and do not filter into the capsule.

questions.ground_truth_ref: glomerular hydrostatic filtration (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: Under normal conditions, essentially all of the filtered glucose is reabsorbed in the:

options.A: Descending limb of the loop of Henle

questions.explanation: The proximal tubule reabsorbs the bulk of filtered solutes, including essentially all glucose (via sodium-glucose cotransport), so that little to no glucose normally appears in the urine.

distractor_notes.A: The descending limb of the loop of Henle is primarily permeable to water, not the site of glucose reabsorption.
distractor_notes.C: The collecting duct fine-tunes water reabsorption under hormonal control, not glucose recovery.
distractor_notes.D: The glomerulus filters glucose into the tubule; it does not reabsorb it.

questions.ground_truth_ref: proximal tubule glucose reabsorption (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: Antidiuretic hormone (ADH, vasopressin) increases water reabsorption chiefly by:

options.A: Actively pumping water out of the filtrate against its gradient
options.B: Decreasing the number of aquaporins in the tubule
options.C: Increasing the permeability of the collecting duct to water
options.D: Blocking sodium reabsorption in the proximal tubule

questions.explanation: ADH promotes insertion of aquaporin water channels into the collecting duct membrane, raising its water permeability so more water is reabsorbed osmotically, concentrating the urine.

distractor_notes.A: Water follows osmotic gradients through channels; it is not actively pumped against its gradient.
distractor_notes.B: ADH increases, not decreases, functional aquaporins in the collecting duct.
distractor_notes.D: ADH's main action is on water permeability, not on blocking proximal sodium reabsorption.

questions.ground_truth_ref: ADH and collecting-duct aquaporins (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: Aldosterone acts on the distal nephron mainly to:

options.A: Increase sodium reabsorption and potassium secretion
options.B: Increase potassium reabsorption and sodium secretion
options.C: Decrease both sodium and water reabsorption
options.D: Directly filter proteins at the glomerulus

questions.explanation: Aldosterone stimulates the distal tubule and collecting duct to reabsorb sodium (with water following) and to secrete potassium, raising blood volume and pressure.

distractor_notes.B: This reverses aldosterone's actions; it reabsorbs sodium and secretes potassium, not the other way around.
distractor_notes.C: Aldosterone increases sodium (and thus water) reabsorption rather than decreasing it.
distractor_notes.D: Aldosterone acts on tubular transport, not on filtering proteins at the glomerulus.

questions.ground_truth_ref: aldosterone Na/K handling (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: A substance is freely filtered at the glomerulus but is neither reabsorbed nor secreted along the tubule. The amount of this substance excreted in the urine per unit time will equal:

options.A: Zero, because everything filtered is recovered
options.B: Twice the amount filtered, due to secretion
options.C: Only the amount actively pumped into the tubule
options.D: The amount filtered at the glomerulus

questions.explanation: If a solute is neither reabsorbed nor secreted, none is added or removed after filtration, so the quantity excreted exactly equals the quantity filtered. This is the basis for using such a marker to estimate the glomerular filtration rate.

distractor_notes.A: Without reabsorption, the filtered solute is not recovered, so excretion is not zero.
distractor_notes.B: There is no secretion of this substance, so excretion cannot exceed what was filtered.
distractor_notes.C: The solute enters the tubule by filtration, not active secretion.

questions.ground_truth_ref: filtered = excreted for inert marker (QBook Ch1) - original framing

passages.title: Enzymes of the digestive tract
passages.passage_text: Digestion breaks large macromolecules into absorbable units through a series of organs and secreted enzymes. Salivary and pancreatic amylases act on starch, gastric and pancreatic proteases act on proteins, and pancreatic lipase, aided by bile, acts on fats. A nutrition class follows a meal containing starch, protein, and triglycerides from the mouth to the small intestine.

questions.stem: Chemical digestion of starch begins in the mouth through the action of:

options.A: Salivary amylase

questions.explanation: Salivary amylase secreted by the salivary glands begins hydrolyzing starch into smaller polysaccharides and maltose in the mouth, before the food reaches the stomach.

distractor_notes.B: Pepsin is a gastric protease that acts on proteins, not starch.
distractor_notes.C: Pancreatic lipase digests fats and acts in the small intestine, not on starch in the mouth.
distractor_notes.D: Trypsin is a pancreatic protease acting on proteins in the small intestine.

questions.ground_truth_ref: salivary amylase starts starch digestion (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: Most enzymatic digestion and nutrient absorption occurs in the:

options.A: Stomach

questions.explanation: The small intestine, using pancreatic enzymes and brush-border enzymes and presenting a large surface area of villi and microvilli, is where most digestion is completed and most nutrients are absorbed.

distractor_notes.A: The stomach mainly begins protein digestion and stores/churns food; little absorption of nutrients occurs there.
distractor_notes.C: The large intestine chiefly reabsorbs water and electrolytes and houses bacteria, not the bulk of enzymatic digestion.
distractor_notes.D: The esophagus is a conduit that moves food to the stomach and performs no chemical digestion.

questions.ground_truth_ref: small intestine digestion/absorption (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: Bile, produced by the liver and stored in the gallbladder, aids fat digestion by:

options.A: Chemically hydrolyzing triglycerides into fatty acids
options.C: Emulsifying large fat globules into smaller droplets, increasing surface area for lipase
options.D: Absorbing fatty acids directly into the blood

questions.explanation: Bile salts are amphipathic and emulsify large fat globules into many small droplets. This greatly increases the surface area available to pancreatic lipase, which does the actual hydrolysis.

distractor_notes.A: Bile does not hydrolyze fats; that is the role of lipase. Bile only emulsifies.
distractor_notes.B: Bile does not neutralize pancreatic enzymes; bicarbonate neutralizes stomach acid to protect them.
distractor_notes.D: Bile does not itself absorb fatty acids; absorption occurs at the intestinal epithelium.

questions.ground_truth_ref: bile emulsification (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: Pancreatic proteases are secreted as inactive zymogens (for example, trypsinogen). The chief advantage of this arrangement is that it:

options.A: Allows the enzymes to work at low temperature
options.C: Eliminates the need for a specific optimal pH
options.D: Prevents the pancreas from digesting its own tissue

questions.explanation: Secreting proteases as inactive zymogens keeps them from digesting the pancreas itself. They are activated only after reaching the intestinal lumen (trypsinogen is cleaved by enteropeptidase to active trypsin).

distractor_notes.A: Zymogen packaging is about controlling where activation occurs, not about temperature tolerance.
distractor_notes.B: Zymogens are still proteins and are not made lipid-soluble by this arrangement.
distractor_notes.C: Each protease still has an optimal pH; the zymogen strategy does not remove that requirement.

questions.ground_truth_ref: zymogen protection of pancreas (QBook Ch1) - original framing

passages.title: Hormonal control of the ovarian cycle
passages.passage_text: The female reproductive cycle is coordinated by hormones from the hypothalamus, anterior pituitary, and ovary. Gonadotropins from the pituitary drive follicle development and ovulation, while the ovary secretes steroid hormones that feed back on the brain and prepare the uterus. A reproductive endocrinology class studies the timing of these signals across a single cycle.

questions.stem: The two anterior pituitary hormones that most directly regulate the ovarian cycle are:

options.A: Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH)

questions.explanation: FSH and LH are the gonadotropins released by the anterior pituitary. FSH promotes follicle maturation and a surge of LH triggers ovulation, making them the direct pituitary regulators of the ovarian cycle.

distractor_notes.B: Insulin and glucagon are pancreatic hormones that regulate blood glucose, not the ovarian cycle.
distractor_notes.C: ADH and oxytocin are released from the posterior pituitary and are not the gonadotropins driving folliculogenesis.
distractor_notes.D: Cortisol and aldosterone are adrenal steroids, not pituitary gonadotropins.

questions.ground_truth_ref: FSH/LH gonadotropins (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: Ovulation is most directly triggered by:

options.A: A drop in FSH to zero
options.B: A sharp surge in luteinizing hormone (LH)

questions.explanation: A mid-cycle surge of LH, itself triggered by rising estrogen from the mature follicle, directly causes the follicle to rupture and release the oocyte (ovulation).

distractor_notes.A: FSH does not fall to zero at ovulation; the immediate trigger is the LH surge, not the absence of FSH.
distractor_notes.C: ADH regulates water balance and has no role in triggering ovulation.
distractor_notes.D: Menstruation is the shedding of the uterine lining and occurs at a different point in the cycle, not the trigger for ovulation.

questions.ground_truth_ref: LH surge triggers ovulation (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: After ovulation, the remnant of the follicle becomes the corpus luteum, which secretes large amounts of progesterone primarily to:

options.A: Trigger the next LH surge immediately
options.C: Maintain the uterine lining in preparation for possible implantation
options.D: Stimulate FSH release to start a new follicle at once

questions.explanation: Progesterone from the corpus luteum maintains and stabilizes the endometrium so that it can support an implanting embryo. If pregnancy does not occur, the corpus luteum regresses, progesterone falls, and the lining is shed.

distractor_notes.A: High progesterone exerts negative feedback that suppresses gonadotropin surges rather than triggering an immediate LH surge.
distractor_notes.B: Progesterone maintains the lining; its withdrawal (a fall in progesterone) is what leads to endometrial breakdown.
distractor_notes.D: Elevated progesterone suppresses FSH-driven recruitment of a new follicle during this phase.

questions.ground_truth_ref: corpus luteum / progesterone (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: Being steroid hormones, estrogen and progesterone differ from peptide hormones in that they:

options.A: Cannot cross the plasma membrane and must bind surface receptors
options.B: Act only through cAMP as a second messenger
options.C: Are synthesized from amino acids by ribosomes
options.D: Are lipid-soluble and typically bind intracellular receptors that regulate gene transcription

questions.explanation: Steroid hormones are lipid-soluble (derived from cholesterol) and diffuse through the plasma membrane to bind intracellular receptors, which then act as transcription factors to alter gene expression.

distractor_notes.A: Peptide hormones, not steroids, are excluded from crossing the membrane and rely on surface receptors.
distractor_notes.B: The cAMP second-messenger pathway is characteristic of many peptide hormones, not the primary mechanism of steroids.
distractor_notes.C: Steroids are synthesized from cholesterol, not translated from amino acids by ribosomes.

questions.ground_truth_ref: steroid hormone mechanism (QBook Ch1) - original framing

passages.title: The autonomic nervous system and a spinal reflex
passages.passage_text: The autonomic nervous system controls involuntary functions through two divisions with largely opposing effects. In a separate demonstration, a physician taps a tendon to elicit a rapid, involuntary muscle contraction, illustrating a simple reflex arc that does not require the brain to initiate the response. Students analyze both the autonomic branches and the components of the reflex.

questions.stem: Activation of the sympathetic division of the autonomic nervous system would most likely produce:

options.A: Increased heart rate and dilated pupils
options.B: Decreased heart rate and increased digestive activity
options.D: Reduced blood flow to skeletal muscle during exercise

questions.explanation: The sympathetic division mediates the fight-or-flight response, raising heart rate, dilating the pupils, and diverting blood toward skeletal muscle while suppressing digestion.

distractor_notes.B: Decreased heart rate and enhanced digestion are parasympathetic (rest-and-digest) effects, not sympathetic.
distractor_notes.C: Pupil constriction and increased salivation are parasympathetic responses.
distractor_notes.D: The sympathetic response increases, not reduces, blood flow to skeletal muscle during exertion.

questions.ground_truth_ref: sympathetic fight-or-flight effects (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: The parasympathetic division is often summarized as promoting which state?

options.A: Fight-or-flight

questions.explanation: The parasympathetic division conserves energy and supports maintenance functions such as digestion and slowing of the heart, commonly summarized as rest-and-digest.

distractor_notes.A: Fight-or-flight is the sympathetic role.
distractor_notes.C: Voluntary skeletal movement is controlled by the somatic motor system, not the autonomic parasympathetic branch.
distractor_notes.D: Conscious sensory perception involves the somatic sensory system and cortex, not the parasympathetic division.

questions.ground_truth_ref: parasympathetic rest-and-digest (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: In the simplest monosynaptic stretch reflex (such as the knee-jerk), the reflex arc is completed with the minimum number of neurons because it involves:

options.A: Three interneurons relaying the signal through the brain
options.B: Only a single motor neuron with no sensory input
options.C: A sensory neuron synapsing directly onto a motor neuron in the spinal cord

questions.explanation: In a monosynaptic reflex, a sensory (afferent) neuron synapses directly on a motor (efferent) neuron in the spinal cord, with no interneuron in between, producing the fastest possible response.

distractor_notes.A: Interneurons and a trip to the brain would add synapses and delay; the monosynaptic reflex avoids both.
distractor_notes.B: A reflex requires sensory input to detect the stretch; a lone motor neuron could not be triggered by the stimulus.
distractor_notes.D: The reflex is mediated by neurons and a synapse, not by direct muscle-to-muscle electrical coupling.

questions.ground_truth_ref: monosynaptic stretch reflex arc (QBook Ch1) - original framing

questions.stem: The fact that the knee-jerk reflex can occur before the brain consciously registers the tap demonstrates that the reflex is integrated in the:

options.A: Cerebral cortex

questions.explanation: The stretch reflex is integrated at the level of the spinal cord, allowing the motor response to be generated locally without waiting for processing in the brain.

distractor_notes.A: Conscious processing in the cerebral cortex occurs after the reflex and is not required to initiate it.
distractor_notes.B: The cerebellum coordinates movement but is not the integration site for this simple spinal reflex.
distractor_notes.C: The tendon is where the stimulus is applied; integration requires a synapse in the central nervous system, i.e., the spinal cord.

questions.ground_truth_ref: spinal integration of reflex (QBook Ch1) - original framing

passages.title: Fatty-acid oxidation and ketone body formation
passages.passage_text: During prolonged fasting, the body shifts toward using fat as fuel. Fatty acids released from adipose tissue are activated and transported into mitochondria, where they are progressively shortened to yield acetyl-CoA. When acetyl-CoA is produced faster than it can be used by the citric acid cycle, the liver converts the excess into ketone bodies that other tissues can use. A metabolism class follows the carbons of a fatty acid from adipose stores to their final oxidation.

questions.stem: The stepwise pathway that shortens a fatty acyl chain by removing two-carbon units is:

options.A: Beta-oxidation

questions.explanation: Beta-oxidation removes two carbons per cycle from the carboxyl end of a fatty acyl-CoA, releasing acetyl-CoA and generating NADH and FADH2 in each round.

distractor_notes.B: Glycolysis oxidizes glucose, not fatty acids.
distractor_notes.C: Fatty acid synthesis builds chains by adding two-carbon units; it is the reverse process.
distractor_notes.D: The pentose phosphate pathway produces NADPH and ribose-5-phosphate, not acetyl-CoA from fatty acids.

questions.ground_truth_ref: beta-oxidation removes two-carbon units (QBook Ch2) - original framing

questions.stem: Each round of beta-oxidation releases which two-carbon product that enters the citric acid cycle?

options.A: Pyruvate

questions.explanation: Each beta-oxidation cycle cleaves off one acetyl-CoA (two carbons), which then condenses with oxaloacetate to enter the citric acid cycle.

distractor_notes.A: Pyruvate comes from glycolysis, not from beta-oxidation of fatty acids.
distractor_notes.C: Oxaloacetate is a citric acid cycle intermediate that accepts acetyl-CoA; it is not the product released by beta-oxidation.
distractor_notes.D: Lactate is a product of anaerobic glycolysis, not fatty acid oxidation.

questions.ground_truth_ref: acetyl-CoA from beta-oxidation (QBook Ch2) - original framing

questions.stem: Long-chain fatty acids must be transported into the mitochondrial matrix for oxidation. This transport requires:

options.A: The electron transport chain
options.D: Simple diffusion through the inner membrane without a carrier

questions.explanation: Long-chain fatty acyl groups cannot freely cross the inner mitochondrial membrane; the carnitine shuttle transfers the acyl group onto carnitine, carries it across, and regenerates fatty acyl-CoA inside the matrix.

distractor_notes.A: The electron transport chain reoxidizes NADH/FADH2; it does not transport fatty acids across the membrane.
distractor_notes.B: The sodium-glucose cotransporter moves glucose across epithelial membranes and has no role in fatty acid entry into mitochondria.
distractor_notes.D: Long-chain acyl-CoA cannot simply diffuse across the inner membrane; it requires the carnitine carrier system.

questions.ground_truth_ref: carnitine shuttle (QBook Ch2) - original framing

questions.stem: Ketone bodies are produced by the liver mainly when:

options.A: Carbohydrate is abundant right after a large meal
options.B: Oxygen is completely absent from the tissue
options.C: Blood glucose is dangerously high due to overeating sugar
options.D: Acetyl-CoA is generated faster than the citric acid cycle can process it, as in prolonged fasting

questions.explanation: During prolonged fasting, high rates of fatty acid oxidation flood the liver with acetyl-CoA while oxaloacetate is diverted to gluconeogenesis. The excess acetyl-CoA is converted into ketone bodies for export to other tissues.

distractor_notes.A: Abundant carbohydrate favors glucose use and storage, suppressing ketogenesis.
distractor_notes.B: Ketone body formation is an aerobic hepatic process, not a response to a complete lack of oxygen (which yields lactate).
distractor_notes.C: Ketogenesis is driven by fasting/low carbohydrate availability, not by high blood glucose from overeating sugar.

questions.ground_truth_ref: ketogenesis during fasting (QBook Ch2) - original framing

questions.stem: Gram for gram, complete oxidation of fatty acids yields more ATP than oxidation of carbohydrates chiefly because fatty acids:

options.A: Are more highly reduced, so their oxidation releases more energy per carbon
options.B: Contain more oxygen atoms per carbon
options.C: Are oxidized without producing any NADH
options.D: Bypass the citric acid cycle entirely

questions.explanation: Fatty acid carbons are more reduced (bear more C-H bonds and less oxygen) than carbohydrate carbons. Oxidizing these more-reduced carbons transfers more electrons to NAD+ and FAD, yielding more ATP per gram.

distractor_notes.B: Fatty acids contain less oxygen per carbon than carbohydrates, which is why they are more reduced and energy-dense.
distractor_notes.C: Beta-oxidation generates abundant NADH (and FADH2); it does not avoid NADH production.
distractor_notes.D: The acetyl-CoA from beta-oxidation is oxidized through the citric acid cycle, not bypassing it.

questions.ground_truth_ref: energy density of fatty acids (QBook Ch2) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: Bacteria and typical eukaryotic cells differ in cellular organization. Which feature is characteristic of bacteria but not of eukaryotic cells?

options.A: A membrane-bound nucleus enclosing the DNA
options.B: A single circular chromosome located in the cytoplasm

discrete_questions.explanation: Bacteria are prokaryotes: they lack a membrane-bound nucleus and typically carry a single circular chromosome in the cytoplasm (nucleoid region). Eukaryotes enclose linear chromosomes within a nucleus.

distractor_notes.A: A membrane-bound nucleus is a eukaryotic feature that bacteria lack.
distractor_notes.C: Membrane-bound mitochondria are found in eukaryotes; bacteria do not have them.
distractor_notes.D: A nuclear envelope with pores is part of the eukaryotic nucleus, absent in bacteria.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: prokaryote vs eukaryote features (QBook Ch1) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: A virus with a positive-sense single-stranded RNA genome enters a host cell. Its genome can most directly be:

options.A: Integrated into host DNA before anything else occurs
options.B: Translated only after being copied into DNA by the host
options.C: Used immediately as mRNA for translation by host ribosomes
options.D: Packaged into new virions without any replication

discrete_questions.explanation: A positive-sense ssRNA genome has the same sense as mRNA, so it can be translated directly by host ribosomes upon entry, producing viral proteins (including a replicase) without a prior conversion step.

distractor_notes.A: Integration into host DNA is characteristic of retroviruses after reverse transcription, not a general step for positive-sense RNA viruses.
distractor_notes.B: Positive-sense RNA does not need to be copied into DNA before translation; that describes retroviruses.
distractor_notes.D: The genome must be replicated to make progeny genomes; simply packaging without replication would not propagate the virus.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: positive-sense ssRNA virus (QBook Ch1) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: During meiosis, a pair of homologous chromosomes fails to separate at anaphase I. This event is called nondisjunction, and it most directly results in gametes with:

options.A: A single point mutation in one gene
options.C: An extra copy of every chromosome (triploidy)

discrete_questions.explanation: Nondisjunction produces gametes with one too many or one too few chromosomes. Fertilization then yields an aneuploid zygote (for example, trisomy or monosomy for the affected chromosome).

distractor_notes.A: A point mutation is a change in a single base pair, unrelated to the missegregation of whole chromosomes.
distractor_notes.B: Telomere shortening is an end-replication phenomenon, not a consequence of nondisjunction.
distractor_notes.C: Triploidy (an entire extra set) arises from errors such as double fertilization, not from failure of a single homologous pair to separate.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: nondisjunction and aneuploidy (QBook Ch1) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: Tendons connect muscle to bone and cartilage cushions the ends of bones. Both tendon and cartilage are examples of:

options.A: Connective tissue

discrete_questions.explanation: Tendons, ligaments, cartilage, bone, and blood are all connective tissues, characterized by cells embedded in an extensive extracellular matrix.

distractor_notes.B: Epithelial tissue lines surfaces and cavities; tendon and cartilage are not epithelia.
distractor_notes.C: Nervous tissue consists of neurons and glia for signaling, not structural connective support.
distractor_notes.D: Smooth muscle is a contractile tissue found in organ walls, distinct from tendon and cartilage.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: connective tissue types (QBook Ch1) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: When core body temperature rises, one homeostatic response of the skin is:

options.A: Vasoconstriction of dermal blood vessels to retain heat
options.B: Vasodilation of dermal blood vessels and sweating to promote heat loss
options.D: Piloerection (raising of hair) to trap an insulating layer of air

discrete_questions.explanation: To lose heat when the body is too warm, dermal blood vessels dilate (bringing warm blood near the surface) and sweat glands secrete sweat, whose evaporation cools the body.

distractor_notes.A: Vasoconstriction conserves heat and is a response to cold, not to overheating.
distractor_notes.C: Shivering generates heat and is a cold response, opposite to what is needed when overheated.
distractor_notes.D: Piloerection traps insulating air to conserve heat, a cold-weather response.

discrete_questions.stem: A person with type O blood is often described as a universal red-cell donor because type O red cells:

options.A: Carry both A and B surface antigens
options.B: Carry neither A nor B surface antigens
options.C: Carry anti-A and anti-B antigens on their surface
options.D: Lack the Rh factor in all cases

discrete_questions.explanation: Type O red cells display neither A nor B antigens, so they are not attacked by the anti-A or anti-B antibodies present in recipients of other ABO types, which is why they can generally be donated to any ABO type.

distractor_notes.A: Cells carrying both A and B antigens are type AB, not type O.
distractor_notes.C: Anti-A and anti-B are antibodies found in plasma, not antigens on the red-cell surface.
distractor_notes.D: ABO donor status is about A/B antigens; Rh status is a separate system and type O is not always Rh-negative.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: ABO blood typing (QBook Ch1) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: Many enzymes require a small organic helper molecule, often derived from a vitamin, to be catalytically active. Such an organic helper is best termed a:

options.A: Substrate

discrete_questions.explanation: A coenzyme is a small organic molecule (frequently vitamin-derived, e.g., NAD+ from niacin) that assists catalysis. Inorganic helpers such as metal ions are called cofactors.

distractor_notes.A: A substrate is the molecule transformed by the enzyme, not a helper that assists catalysis.
distractor_notes.B: A metal ion is an inorganic cofactor; the question specifies an organic, vitamin-derived helper, which is a coenzyme.
distractor_notes.D: An allosteric inhibitor reduces enzyme activity; it is not a required organic catalytic helper.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: coenzyme vs cofactor / vitamins (QBook Ch2) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: An allosteric enzyme is inhibited by the end product of its pathway, which binds at a site distinct from the active site. This regulatory strategy is best described as:

options.A: Competitive inhibition at the active site

discrete_questions.explanation: When a pathway's end product binds a regulatory (allosteric) site on an early enzyme and reduces its activity, the pathway is turned down as product accumulates. This is feedback inhibition, a common allosteric control.

distractor_notes.A: Competitive inhibition involves binding at the active site; here the inhibitor binds a separate regulatory site.
distractor_notes.B: Irreversible covalent modification permanently alters the enzyme; allosteric feedback is reversible binding.
distractor_notes.C: Substrate-level activation is not the described phenomenon; the end product is inhibiting, not activating.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: allosteric feedback inhibition (QBook Ch2) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: A single nucleotide in DNA is composed of:

options.A: A nitrogenous base, a deoxyribose sugar, and one or more phosphate groups
options.C: Two nitrogenous bases joined by a hydrogen bond
options.D: A ribose sugar and a phosphate, with no base

discrete_questions.explanation: A DNA nucleotide consists of a nitrogenous base, a five-carbon deoxyribose sugar, and a phosphate group. The base plus sugar alone (without phosphate) is a nucleoside.

distractor_notes.B: A base alone is not a complete nucleotide; the sugar and phosphate are also required.
distractor_notes.C: Base pairing by hydrogen bonds occurs between strands; it does not define a single nucleotide's composition.
distractor_notes.D: DNA nucleotides use deoxyribose (not ribose) and must include a nitrogenous base.

discrete_questions.stem: For the exothermic gas-phase equilibrium N2 + 3 H2 in equilibrium with 2 NH3, increasing the temperature at constant volume will shift the equilibrium:

options.A: Toward the products (more NH3)
options.C: In neither direction, since temperature has no effect
options.D: Toward the side with fewer gas molecules

discrete_questions.explanation: For an exothermic reaction, heat is effectively a product. By Le Chatelier's principle, adding heat (raising the temperature) shifts the equilibrium toward the reactants, reducing the yield of ammonia.

distractor_notes.A: Raising temperature favors the endothermic (reverse) direction here, decreasing product, not increasing it.
distractor_notes.C: Temperature changes do shift equilibria and also change the equilibrium constant.
distractor_notes.D: A shift toward fewer gas molecules is a response to increased pressure, not to increased temperature.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: Le Chatelier temperature effect (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: In the sulfate ion, SO4^2-, what is the oxidation number of the sulfur atom? (Oxygen is -2.)
discrete_questions.explanation: Four oxygens contribute 4 x (-2) = -8. The overall ion charge is -2, so sulfur must be +6 because +6 + (-8) = -2.

distractor_notes.A: +2 for sulfur would give an overall charge of +2 - 8 = -6, not -2.
distractor_notes.B: +4 for sulfur would give +4 - 8 = -4, not the -2 charge of sulfate.
distractor_notes.D: -2 for sulfur would give -2 - 8 = -10, far from the actual -2 ion charge.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: oxidation number assignment (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: Adding a specific volume of concentrated strong acid to a beaker of pure water and stirring is best described as which type of change to the solution's composition?

options.A: A shift that increases the hydroxide ion concentration and raises the pH
options.B: No change in pH, because water buffers the acid completely
options.C: A change that raises the pH above 7
options.D: A shift that increases the hydrogen ion concentration and lowers the pH

discrete_questions.explanation: A strong acid dissociates completely, adding hydrogen ions to the solution. Increasing the hydrogen ion concentration lowers the pH, making the solution more acidic.

distractor_notes.A: Adding acid raises hydrogen ion concentration and lowers pH; hydroxide is consumed, not increased.
distractor_notes.B: Pure water is not a buffer, so it cannot neutralize added strong acid; the pH does change.
distractor_notes.C: Adding acid drives the pH below 7, not above it.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: strong acid effect on pH (QBook Ch3) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: A carboxylic acid reacts with an alcohol under acid catalysis to form a product with a characteristic pleasant odor plus water. This product functional group is an:

options.A: Ester

discrete_questions.explanation: The condensation of a carboxylic acid with an alcohol (Fischer esterification) yields an ester (R-CO-O-R') and water. Esters are known for their fruity odors.

distractor_notes.B: An amide forms from a carboxylic acid and an amine, not an alcohol.
distractor_notes.C: An ether (R-O-R') lacks the carbonyl of an ester and is not the product of acid-plus-alcohol condensation.
distractor_notes.D: A ketone has a carbonyl flanked by two carbons and is not produced by esterification.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: Fischer esterification / functional groups (QBook Ch7) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: Two compounds share the molecular formula C4H10 but differ: one is n-butane (a straight chain) and the other is isobutane (a branched chain). These two compounds are best classified as:

options.A: Enantiomers
options.D: The same compound in different conformations

discrete_questions.explanation: n-Butane and isobutane have the same molecular formula but different connectivity of their atoms, which defines constitutional (structural) isomers.

distractor_notes.A: Enantiomers are nonsuperimposable mirror images with identical connectivity, not different connectivity.
distractor_notes.C: Diastereomers are stereoisomers with the same connectivity that are not mirror images; these differ in connectivity.
distractor_notes.D: Conformations differ only by rotation about bonds with identical connectivity; these are distinct molecules.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: constitutional vs stereoisomers (QBook Ch7) - original framing

discrete_questions.stem: In fungi, the cell wall is composed largely of chitin, whereas plant cell walls are composed of cellulose. This distinction reflects that fungi:

options.A: Are prokaryotic organisms lacking a nucleus
options.C: Are eukaryotes but build their walls from a nitrogen-containing polysaccharide rather than cellulose
options.D: Lack any cell wall at all

discrete_questions.explanation: Fungi are eukaryotes whose cell walls are made of chitin, a nitrogen-containing polysaccharide (a polymer of N-acetylglucosamine), distinguishing them from the cellulose walls of plants.

distractor_notes.A: Fungi are eukaryotes with true nuclei, not prokaryotes.
distractor_notes.B: Fungi are heterotrophs and do not photosynthesize; cellulose is a plant wall polymer, not a fungal one.
distractor_notes.D: Fungi do have a cell wall; it is simply made of chitin rather than cellulose.

discrete_questions.ground_truth_ref: fungal chitin cell wall (QBook Ch1) - original framing
